title: UI Responsiveness & RecyclerView vs Compose
description: Frame budgets, jank sources, layout performance, and RecyclerView pool-and-rebind vs LazyColumn compose-on-demand

references.title: Android Developers — Lazy lists in Compose
Android Developers — Performance in Compose lists

lesson:
Part 1 — Frame Budget & Jank

The 16ms Frame Budget

At 60 fps the display refreshes every 16.67 ms. Each frame must complete a fixed pipeline before the next VSYNC signal fires:

VSYNC tick (every 16.67 ms) │ ├─ 1. Input handling (touch events, key events) ├─ 2. Animation evaluation (ObjectAnimator, ValueAnimator, Compose animate*) ├─ 3. Measure (View.onMeasure / LayoutNode.measure) ├─ 4. Layout (View.onLayout / LayoutNode.place) ├─ 5. Draw (Canvas commands → display list / Compose draw phase) └─ 6. GPU render + swap (RenderThread sends display list to GPU → SurfaceFlinger) If steps 1-6 exceed 16 ms → frame is DROPPED → user sees "jank"

Choreographer schedules this pipeline. doFrame() fires once per VSYNC. If your work takes 25 ms, the next frame is skipped entirely — the user sees a stutter. Two missed frames (32 ms) is noticeable; three or more is clearly broken.

90/120 fps devices: On a 90 Hz display the budget shrinks to 11.1 ms. On 120 Hz it's 8.3 ms. The same code that felt smooth at 60 fps may jank at 120 fps.

What Steals Frame Time

CulpritCostWhy
GC pauses2-5 ms per minor GCART must stop-the-world briefly; allocating many short-lived objects in onDraw/onBind triggers this
Layout inflation1-10 ms per inflateXML parsing, reflection to instantiate Views — never inflate inside onBindViewHolder
Nested RelativeLayoutExponentialRelativeLayout always double-measures children; nested 3 deep = 2^3 = 8 measure passes
Main-thread I/OUnboundedSharedPreferences.getAll(), database queries, file reads — can block 50-500 ms
OverdrawGPU boundDrawing the same pixel 3-4 times wastes fill rate; enable "Show GPU Overdraw" in Developer Options
Large bitmap decode5-50 msDecoding a 4000x3000 JPEG on the main thread; use BitmapFactory.Options.inSampleSize or Coil/Glide off-thread

View System Layout Performance — Measure Passes

The number of measure passes a layout performs determines how expensive it is, especially when nested:

LayoutMeasure PassesNotes
LinearLayout1 passSimple: measure each child once, stack them
LinearLayout with weights2 passesFirst pass measures natural sizes, second pass distributes remaining space by weight
RelativeLayoutAlways 2 passesHorizontal rules first, then vertical rules — must measure twice to resolve relative constraints
ConstraintLayout1 passSolves all constraints in a single pass using a linear constraint solver (Cassowary-like)
Nesting multiplies exponentially: If a 2-pass layout contains another 2-pass layout, the inner one is measured 2 times by its parent, each time doing 2 passes internally → 4 total. Three levels deep: 2^3 = 8 passes. This is why deeply nested RelativeLayouts cause severe jank.

ConstraintLayout was built to replace nested RelativeLayouts — it achieves complex layouts in a flat hierarchy with one pass.

Compose Single-Pass Layout Enforcement

Compose enforces a strict rule: each child can only be measured once. If your custom layout calls measurable.measure() a second time on the same child, Compose throws an IllegalStateException:

// This CRASHES at runtime: Layout(content = content) { measurables, constraints -> val first = measurables[0].measure(constraints) // OK val second = measurables[0].measure(constraints) // IllegalStateException! layout(0, 0) {} }

This is intentional. By making double-measurement impossible, Compose guarantees O(n) layout performance regardless of hierarchy depth. If you need to measure with different constraints, use SubcomposeLayout or intrinsic measurements (which add a pre-pass but don't break the single-measure rule).

Part 2 — RecyclerView vs LazyColumn

RecyclerView: Pool-and-Rebind Model

RecyclerView Lifecycle: 1. CREATION — onCreateViewHolder() — inflate XML, find views │ (expensive: ~5-10 ms per item type) │ ▼ 2. POOL — RecycledViewPool holds detached ViewHolders by viewType │ (one pool per viewType, default max 5) │ ▼ 3. BIND — onBindViewHolder(holder, position) — set data on existing views │ (cheap: ~0.5-1 ms, no inflation) │ ▼ 4. VISIBLE — on screen, attached to RecyclerView │ ▼ 5. DETACH — scrolls off → goes BACK to pool (step 2) └─ Pool full? → ViewHolder is DESTROYED (GC'd)

The key insight: creation is expensive, binding is cheap. The pool ensures you pay the creation cost only a few times per view type. During a fast fling, RecyclerView just rebinds existing ViewHolders — no inflation, no GC pressure, steady frame rate.

DiffUtil + Partial Payloads

DiffUtil computes the minimal set of changes between two lists on a background thread. Combined with partial payloads, you can update only the specific views that changed within a ViewHolder — no full rebind needed.

// 1. Define the DiffUtil.ItemCallback class UserDiffCallback : DiffUtil.ItemCallback<User>() { override fun areItemsTheSame(old: User, new: User): Boolean { return old.id == new.id // same identity? } override fun areContentsTheSame(old: User, new: User): Boolean { return old == new // data class equals — all fields } // 2. Return WHICH fields changed (partial payload) override fun getChangePayload(old: User, new: User): Any? { val changes = mutableSetOf<String>() if (old.name != new.name) changes.add("name") if (old.avatar != new.avatar) changes.add("avatar") if (old.status != new.status) changes.add("status") return changes.ifEmpty { null } // null = full rebind } } // 3. In the Adapter — handle partial payloads override fun onBindViewHolder( holder: UserViewHolder, position: Int, payloads: MutableList<Any> // non-empty = partial update ) { if (payloads.isEmpty()) { // Full bind — set all views onBindViewHolder(holder, position) return } val changes = payloads[0] as Set<*> val user = getItem(position) // Only update the views whose data actually changed if ("name" in changes) holder.nameView.text = user.name if ("avatar" in changes) Glide.with(holder.avatar).load(user.avatar).into(holder.avatar) if ("status" in changes) holder.statusBadge.setStatus(user.status) }
Why partial payloads matter: Without payloads, changing a user's online status rebinds the entire ViewHolder — including reloading the avatar image. With payloads, you only toggle the status badge. On a chat list with 100 items changing status frequently, this eliminates dozens of unnecessary image loads per second.

LazyColumn: Compose-on-Demand Model

LazyColumn Lifecycle: 1. COMPOSE — item enters viewport → composable function runs │ (creates LayoutNodes, runs effects, allocates state) │ ▼ 2. VISIBLE — on screen, actively composed │ ▼ 3. DISPOSE — scrolls off → composable LEAVES composition entirely │ (DisposableEffect onDispose runs, state lost unless remembered) │ ▼ 4. RE-ENTRY — scrolls back → composable runs FROM SCRATCH (no pool, no rebind — full recomposition) There is NO pool. There is NO ViewHolder cache. Every appearance is a fresh composition.

What key Does (and Doesn't Do)

A common misconception: key does NOT enable recycling. LazyColumn has no recycling. What key does:

LazyColumn { items(users, key = { it.id }) { user -> UserCard(user) // state inside UserCard survives REORDERING } } // Without key: items identified by INDEX // List [A, B, C] → delete A → [B, C] // Index 0 was A, now it's B → Compose thinks item 0 changed // → B gets A's remembered state (wrong!) // With key = { it.id }: // Compose knows B is still B regardless of position // → B keeps its own state, A's state is disposed
key is for identity, not caching. It tells Compose which item is which when the list is reordered, inserted into, or deleted from. It does NOT keep items alive off-screen. Items off-screen are still disposed and recomposed from scratch when they re-enter.

Performance Comparison

ScenarioRecyclerViewLazyColumn
First appearanceInflate + bind (~5-10 ms first time, ~1 ms rebind)Full composition (~2-5 ms, depends on composable complexity)
Data changeDiffUtil + partial payload → update only changed viewsRecomposition of affected items — skips unchanged composables if inputs are stable
Fast flingPool provides pre-created ViewHolders → consistent bind-only costMust compose from scratch for each new item entering viewport → more GC, higher variance
MemoryPool holds ~5 ViewHolders per type (fixed ceiling)Only visible items exist — lower baseline, but allocation spikes on fast scroll
State preservationManual: save/restore in bind/recycleAutomatic with rememberSaveable + key

When Each Wins

RecyclerView wins when: ───────────────────── • Fast fling performance is critical (social feeds, messaging) • Partial payloads save significant work (chat status badges, like counts) • Constrained hardware (low-RAM devices, Wear OS with limited CPU) • Mixed view types with expensive creation (media players, maps) • You need prefetching control (LayoutManager.setItemPrefetchEnabled) LazyColumn wins when: ───────────────────── • State management matters (expandable items, inline editing, selections) • Simpler code is more valuable than peak scroll perf • Items are lightweight composables (text-only lists, settings screens) • You're already fully in Compose (no interop overhead) • Animations and item transitions (animateItem modifier)

questions.title: Frame Budget at 60 fps
questions.description: At 60 fps, what is the approximate time budget for each frame, and what happens if you exceed it?
options: 33 ms per frame; exceeding it causes the frame to render at half quality
16.67 ms per frame; exceeding it causes the frame to be dropped (jank)
16.67 ms per frame; exceeding it causes the system to slow down the animation to compensate
8.3 ms per frame; exceeding it triggers a background thread to help render
questions.explanation: At 60 fps, the display refreshes every 1000 ms / 60 = 16.67 ms. Choreographer fires doFrame() on each VSYNC. If your frame processing (input → animation → measure → layout → draw → GPU) takes longer than 16.67 ms, the frame is dropped entirely — the display shows the previous frame again. The user perceives this as a stutter or "jank". There is no graceful degradation; it's all-or-nothing per frame.
hints: 1000 ms / 60 frames = ?
The system doesn't slow down — it skips

questions.title: Layout Measure Passes
questions.description: You have a RelativeLayout containing a LinearLayout with layout_weight, which itself contains another RelativeLayout. How many measure passes does the innermost RelativeLayout undergo?
options: 2 passes — each layout measures independently
4 passes — RelativeLayout(2) x LinearLayout-weight(2), inner RelativeLayout measures itself 2 more times but that doesn't multiply
8 passes — outer RelativeLayout(2) x weighted LinearLayout(2) x inner RelativeLayout(2) = 2^3
6 passes — the passes add, not multiply: 2 + 2 + 2
questions.explanation: Measure passes multiply through nesting. The outer RelativeLayout measures its child (the LinearLayout) 2 times. Each time, the weighted LinearLayout measures its child 2 times (once for natural size, once for weight distribution). Each time, the inner RelativeLayout does 2 passes internally. Total: 2 x 2 x 2 = 8 measure passes for the innermost views. This exponential blowup is why ConstraintLayout (1 pass, flat hierarchy) was created.
hints: Think about what happens when a parent measures a child — the child runs its FULL measure logic each time
RelativeLayout = 2, weighted LinearLayout = 2 — how does nesting combine these?

questions.title: Compose Double Measure
questions.description: In a Compose custom Layout, what happens if you call measurable.measure(constraints) twice on the same child?
options: The second call returns a cached result from the first measurement
The second call re-measures with the new constraints, replacing the first result
Compose throws an IllegalStateException at runtime
The compiler prevents this — it won't compile
questions.explanation: Compose enforces a strict single-measure rule: each measurable can only be measured once per layout pass. Calling measure() a second time throws IllegalStateException. This is a deliberate design choice to guarantee O(n) layout performance. Unlike the View system where double-measuring is common (RelativeLayout, weighted LinearLayout), Compose makes it structurally impossible. If you need multiple measurement passes, use SubcomposeLayout or intrinsic measurements.
hints: Compose's layout system was designed to prevent the exponential blowup that plagues the View system
This is a runtime check, not a compile-time one

questions.title: DiffUtil getChangePayload
questions.description: What does getChangePayload accomplish in this DiffUtil callback, and how does it affect the Adapter?
options: It has no effect — DiffUtil ignores getChangePayload and always does a full rebind
It identifies WHICH fields changed so the Adapter can update only those specific views instead of rebinding the entire ViewHolder
It caches the old data so the adapter can animate between old and new values
It tells DiffUtil to skip items where only minor fields changed
questions.explanation: When areItemsTheSame returns true (same item) but areContentsTheSame returns false (data changed), DiffUtil calls getChangePayload. The returned object (here, a set of field names) is passed to the 3-argument onBindViewHolder as a payload. The adapter checks which fields changed and updates only those views. Without payloads, changing a message's read count would rebind the entire row — reloading the avatar, re-rendering the message text, etc. With payloads, only the read count text view is updated.
hints: Think about what happens to the payloads parameter in the 3-argument onBindViewHolder
Consider what work you SKIP by only updating changed fields

questions.title: LazyColumn Disposal
questions.description: In LazyColumn, what happens to an item's composable state when it scrolls off-screen and then scrolls back?
options: The state is preserved in a pool like RecyclerView — the composable resumes where it left off
The composable is disposed when it leaves the viewport and recomposed from scratch when it re-enters — state is lost unless explicitly saved
The composable stays composed but becomes invisible — no disposal happens
Compose keeps the last 5 items cached, like RecyclerView's pool size
questions.explanation: LazyColumn has no pool and no cache. When an item scrolls off-screen, its composable leaves composition entirely — DisposableEffect cleanup runs, remembered state is discarded. When it scrolls back, the composable function runs from scratch: all remember { } blocks re-initialize, effects restart. To preserve state across disposal, use rememberSaveable combined with a stable key.
hints: LazyColumn's model is fundamentally different from RecyclerView
Think about what 'leaving composition' means for remember { } blocks

questions.title: What key Does in LazyColumn
questions.description: What does providing a key to items() in a LazyColumn accomplish?
options: It enables ViewHolder-style recycling — items with the same key reuse each other's composition
It preserves item state across reordering by associating remembered state with the key instead of the positional index
It tells Compose to cache off-screen items with that key for faster re-entry
It groups items by key for section headers
questions.explanation: key provides a stable identity for items. Without it, Compose identifies items by index. If you delete item 0 from a list [A, B, C], item B moves to index 0 and inherits A's remembered state — clearly wrong. With key = { it.id }, Compose knows B is B regardless of its position. B keeps its own state, and A's state is properly disposed. key does NOT enable recycling or caching. Off-screen items are still disposed; key only ensures the correct state is associated with the correct item while it IS composed.
hints: Think about what happens to state when a list item changes position
key is about identity, not performance

questions.title: Frame Budget Stealers
questions.description: Name at least four things that commonly steal frame time on the main thread and explain why each is problematic for the 16 ms budget.
questions.answer: Common frame-time stealers: (1) GC pauses — allocating many short-lived objects (e.g., in onDraw or onBind) triggers garbage collection, causing 2-5 ms stop-the-world pauses. (2) Layout inflation — inflating XML views involves parsing and reflection, costing 1-10 ms; inflating inside onBindViewHolder is especially bad. (3) Nested double-measure layouts — RelativeLayout always measures twice, and nesting multiplies exponentially (2^n), turning a simple layout into dozens of measure passes. (4) Main-thread I/O — SharedPreferences.getAll(), database queries, or file reads can block 50-500 ms, freezing the entire UI. (5) Overdraw — drawing the same pixel multiple times wastes GPU fill rate. (6) Large bitmap decoding — decoding a multi-megapixel image synchronously can take 5-50 ms.
questions.explanation: All six of these share one trait: they consume time on the main thread during the frame pipeline. Since Choreographer runs input, animation, measure, layout, and draw sequentially on the main thread, any one of these can push the total past 16 ms and drop a frame. The fix is different for each: object pooling or avoiding allocations for GC, ViewStub or AsyncLayoutInflater for inflation, ConstraintLayout for nesting, coroutines/IO dispatcher for blocking I/O, removing redundant backgrounds for overdraw, and Coil/Glide for bitmap loading.
hints: Think about what happens during each phase: measure, layout, draw, and between frames
Some steal CPU time, some steal GPU time

questions.title: RecyclerView Partial Payload
questions.description: This adapter reloads the entire ViewHolder (including an expensive image) every time any field changes. Fix it using partial payloads so that changing only the like count does NOT reload the image.

checks.message: Override getChangePayload to identify which fields changed
Handle the payloads parameter in onBindViewHolder

questions.explanation: By overriding getChangePayload, DiffUtil tells the adapter exactly which fields changed. The 3-argument onBindViewHolder checks the payloads list: if non-empty, it only updates the specific views that correspond to changed fields. Now when a user taps "like" and only the like count changes, Glide does NOT reload the image — saving network, memory, and frame time.
hints: DiffUtil has a third callback method that returns WHAT changed between two items
onBindViewHolder has a 3-argument overload that receives payloads

questions.title: RecyclerView vs LazyColumn — Fast Fling
questions.description: During a fast fling on a list with complex items (images, styled text, multiple view types), which approach generally provides smoother scroll performance and why?
options: LazyColumn — Compose's single-pass layout is always faster than the View system
RecyclerView — its ViewHolder pool provides pre-created views that only need cheap rebinding, avoiding the allocation spike of composing from scratch
They perform identically — both use the same underlying rendering pipeline
LazyColumn — it disposes off-screen items, freeing memory and reducing GC pressure
questions.explanation: During fast fling, new items enter the viewport rapidly. RecyclerView pulls pre-created ViewHolders from its pool and only runs onBindViewHolder (~0.5-1 ms). LazyColumn must compose each item from scratch — running the composable function, creating LayoutNodes, allocating state, running effects (~2-5 ms). This per-item cost adds up during fast scrolling. RecyclerView's pool acts as a buffer that amortizes creation cost, while LazyColumn pays it on every entry. For simple items the difference is negligible, but for complex items (images, nested layouts, multiple text styles), RecyclerView's pool-and-rebind model provides more consistent frame timing during aggressive scrolling.
hints: Think about what work each system does when a NEW item enters the viewport
One reuses pre-created objects, the other creates from scratch

questions.title: ConstraintLayout vs Nested RelativeLayout
questions.description: An engineer replaces this nested layout with a flat ConstraintLayout. What is the performance impact?
questions.code:
<!-- BEFORE: Nested RelativeLayouts -->
<RelativeLayout>              <!-- 2 passes -->
    <RelativeLayout>          <!-- measured 2x by parent, 2 passes each = 4 -->
        <RelativeLayout>      <!-- measured 4x by parent, 2 passes each = 8 -->
            <TextView />
            <ImageView />
        </RelativeLayout>
    </RelativeLayout>
</RelativeLayout>

<!-- AFTER: Flat ConstraintLayout -->
<ConstraintLayout>            <!-- 1 pass -->
    <TextView />
    <ImageView />
</ConstraintLayout>
options: No meaningful difference — modern devices are fast enough that layout passes don't matter
Measure passes drop from 8 to 1 — an 8x reduction in layout work for the leaf views
ConstraintLayout is actually slower because its constraint solver is more complex
The improvement is only 2x because ConstraintLayout still needs 4 passes for complex constraints
questions.explanation: Three levels of nested RelativeLayout means the innermost views are measured 2^3 = 8 times. A flat ConstraintLayout solves all constraints in a single pass using a linear constraint solver. That's an 8x reduction in measure work for the leaf views. In a RecyclerView where this layout is used for each item, this difference is multiplied by every visible item — potentially turning a janky scroll into a smooth one. This is exactly why ConstraintLayout was created: to achieve complex relative positioning without the exponential cost of nested RelativeLayouts.
hints: Calculate 2^3 for the nested case
ConstraintLayout was specifically designed to solve this problem
